title: China, Germany And Eastern Europe’s Factory Hub
text:
China, Germany And Eastern Europe’s Factory Hub

August Gudmundsson

Gavekal Research | 29 Jun 2026

There is something close to panic among European policymakers over the prospect of a second “China shock” wiping out whole swathes of the continent’s manufacturing industries (see Europe’s China Shock In Perspective). Few regions seem as exposed as the Visegrád Four of Poland, Czechia, Hungary and Slovakia, whose growth model has long centered on serving as Europe’s low-cost manufacturing hub, anchored by German demand. Yet the evidence suggests the conventional narrative is incomplete. While China is challenging the region’s old “workbench” manufacturing model, it is also helping to reshape it through inputs, investment and supply-chain integration.Both forces can be seen in the data. China’s industrial rise threatens Eastern Europe’s low-cost manufacturing advantage, while Germany’s industrial downturn is weighing on demand. Yet on closer inspection, the V4 group appears to be facing a transitory midlife crisis rather than imminent decrepitude. China is also helping to push the region up the value chain, while Germany’s competitiveness pressures may ultimately spur firms to shift more production east, not less.
